prompt: What am I waiting on from my team?

score.finalText:
You’re waiting on a few real things from your team, and the clearest blockers are these:

- **Richard Ellison** — share the **90% drawings** with you. Due **2026-05-01**, still open. [Source: Action Items & Insights]
- **Brandon Griffith** — fix the **Om Nangia signature** issue and send back the updated document. Due **2026-05-05**, still open, and it shows up twice from the same thread, so that one looks unresolved. [Source: Action Items & Insights]
- **Corey Kunkleman** — give you status on whether the **insurance requirements are met** so you can respond to the client. Due **2026-05-05**, still open, also duplicated in the system. **Project 31.** [Source: Action Items & Insights]
- **Bob Wright** — create the **design-cost change request** under the existing Steel Services PO for added **Vermillion Rise** design costs. Still open. [Source: Action Items & Insights]
- **Maria Calcetero** — go back to the charter companies with the **$7,500 Piper offer**. Still open. [Source: Action Items & Insights]
- **Spencer Geissel** — review the attached **sprinkler options** for **Superior Beverage**. Still open, also duplicated. [Source: Action Items & Insights]

What matters most: the overdue items are mostly admin/compliance handoffs, not field production — but they’re the kind that slow your response time and make you the bottleneck if they sit.

There’s also a second layer: communication-derived follow-ups that look like unresolved team dependencies, especially on **Westfield Collective**. Those are not clean task-register items, but they show repeated unresolved pressure around:
- completion list / getting people off site,
- schedule dates not being clearly tracked,
- locks/security,
- landscaping sequencing,
- punch and closeout coordination.  
All of that is still surfacing as unresolved risk signal in the system. [Source: Action Items & Insights]

One thing that’s directly on your side too:
- **RFI #2** — “Specified fire rating for north wall assembly” is open, overdue, and the ball is in **Brandon**. Due **2026-04-10**. [Source: Action Items & Insights]

My read: you’re not mainly waiting on broad effort from the team — you’re waiting on **specific confirmations and document handoffs**. The highest-value chases today are:
1. **Corey** on insurance status
2. **Brandon Griffith** on signature correction
3. **Richard** on 90% drawings
4. **Bob** on Vermillion design-cost change request

If you want, I can turn this into a **clean “waiting on others” list by person and project** or draft **Teams follow-up messages** to each of them.

Key participants included John Smith, Project Manager; Sarah Johnson, Lead Engineer; Michael Lee, Procurement Officer; and Lisa Chen, Facilities Manager. The meeting aimed to ensure alignment among team members and address any immediate concerns related to the project timeline and budget.

Brandon Clymer pointed out that the reliance on subcontractors, coupled with a scarcity of internal project leads, puts pressure on workforce management and job execution. Currently, there are approximately 20 ongoing projects with over 30 staff involved, indicating a lean operational scale. The Kentucky project is set to begin dirt work in July, with specific roofing plans involving standing seam metal panels, which aims to streamline construction by eliminating the typical sub-roof installation. Regular biweekly meetings with Symbotic were proposed to ensure stakeholder alignment, and proactive communication with the landlord will be maintained to facilitate timely approvals.
